0: Jens Lansche Joachim Müller|Research Article Universität Hohenheim, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Stuttgart, Germany|Life cycle assessment of energy generation of biogas fed combined heat and power plants: Environmental impact of different agricultural substrates

The utilization of agricultural biomass for anaerobic digestion is increasing in Ger- many since the first version of the Renewable Energies Sources Act (EEG) in 2000. Main products of this conversion process are biogas and digestate, whereby the biogas is mainly used for generation of heat and electricity in combined heat and power plants (CHP). This study investigated the potential environmental impact of anaerobic digestion processes with different agricultural substrates by the life cycle assessment (LCA) method. It focuses on liquid manure and energy crops as feedstock on the one hand and a comparison of four virtual model plants on the other hand. Besides global warming potential (GWP), the impact categories eutrophication po- tential (EP) and acidification potential (AP) are presented in this work. The results show that greenhouse gas emissions can be reduced with anaerobic digestion of liquid manure as well as energy crops, particularly when digestate storage tanks are gas-tight. When energy crops are fermented together with liquid manure, the biggest credit for the avoidance of greenhouse gas emissions was given for the generation of electricity. The results differ from those of GWP when looking at the AP and the EP. These impact categories show similar results with a reduction of emissions for liquid manure in mono-digestion but increasing emissions for digestion of energy crops together with 0-35% liquid manure.
Keywords: Digestate storage / Greenhouse gas emissions / Global warming potential / LCA Anaerobic digestion

Abbreviations: AP, acidification potential; CO2 eq., carbon dioxide equivelants; CHP, combined heat and power plant; EEG, renewable energies source act; EP, eutrophication potential; FU, functional unit; GHG, greenhouse gas; GWP, global warming potential; LCA, life cycle assessment

emission of CH4 related to manure management accounted for 260,000 tons of agricultural GHG emissions in Germany [2]. Anaerobic digestion of liquid manure shows a reduction po- tential for these emissions [3,4]. Furthermore, emissions from conventional production systems (e.g. fossil fuel power plants) are avoided by substitution when the produced biogas is used in a combined heat and power plant (CHP) for generation of electricity and heat [5,6]. Biogas plants can use liquid manure in co-digestion with energy crops [7,8] as well as energy crops or manure in mono-digestion [9, 10]. The production of biogas is increasing considerably in Germany since the Renewable Ener- gies Sources Act (EEG) entered into force in the year 2000. One major objective of this law is to contribute to climate protection by encouraging a sustainable development of energy supply in Germany [11]. Due to this the number of biogas plants increased from 1050 to 4984, while the installed electrical power enhanced from 65 to 1893 MW in the last decade [12]. Since the second amendment of the EEG in 2009, biogas plants with an electrical

power of maximum 150 kW, which use manure with a feedstock share of at least 30%, get a financial incentive of 0.04 €/kWh for the electricity produced [11]. Life cycle assessment (LCA) in general is used to evaluate the potential environmental impacts throughout the life cycle of a product, which means including the phases of raw material extraction, production, usage, and disposal [13]. Several studies on the assessment of environmen- tal impacts from biogas production systems with LCA method have been published for Denmark, Sweden, and Germany in the last years [14-21]. All those studies report a reduction of global warming potential (GWP) by biogas production. How- ever, they were based on different functional units (FU) and system boundaries and hence came to different results in the ab- solute amount of potential GWP reductions. The influence of the digestate storage on GWP can also be quite significant. A research at 61 biogas plants in Germany has analyzed the gas production potential of biogas digestates. For this purpose, the effluent of single-stage as well as multi-stage biogas plants was tested in lab- oratory at 20-22°, respectively 37°C for at least 60 days until gas production decreased significantly. The results ranged from 0.1% (20-22°C, multi-stage) to 21.8% (37°C, single-stage) of total CH4 yield [22]. Liebetrau et al. [23] have measured emis- sions from uncovered digestate storage tanks in a range from 1.4 to 10.4% of total CH4 production with a mean value of 4.2%. Gärtner et al. [18] assess the CH4-emissions from an uncovered digestate storage tank in a range from 2.5 to 15%. Nonetheless, the uncertainties in literature values make it difficult to specify the influence of uncovered digestate storage on GWP of biogas production systems. Furthermore, studies have to be conducted to elucidate whether the incentive for manure digestion in the EEG leads to considerable reductions of GHG emissions or not. Therefore, this study uses LCA to illustrate to which amount biogas production from agricultural feedstock can contribute to a reduction of environmental impacts under German conditions taking into consideration GHG emissions as well as emissions, which contribute to acidification and eutrophication.

6: The research presented here is part of a study which has the aim to compare several biogas systems with conventional systems for provision of energy including different utilization pathways of the biogas from an environmental point of view to identify the systems which have the biggest potential to reduce nega- tive environmental impacts. Therefore, the main function of the biogas system is considered as reducing the negative environ- mental impacts while producing energy for the substitution of conventional energy systems. In this publication the results of the utilization of biogas for power and heat production in CHPs are presented. Further research will include the upgrading of biogas to biomethane as well as different utilization pathways of biomethane. Hence a FU was chosen which allows the compari- son of these systems. The EEG 2009 promotes renewable energies in Germany. One main purpose of this law is to optimize energy provision in the meaning of a better protection of climate and environment. An additional financial incentive is provided for

the utilization of at least 30% of manure as feedstock in biogas plants. The environmental impact of this incentive for manure digestion has not been under consideration yet in scientific re- search. Therefore, the assessment of this aspect is an essential element in the research presented here.

8: The method of LCA is standardized in ISO 14000 series since more than 10 years. This study is based on the latest version of this standard (DIN EN ISO 14040 and 14044) [13,24 with the exception that no critical review was conducted. A model for biogas production at four "virtual" biogas plants was built with the GaBi software using the ecoinvent-database and data from a research biogas plant of the University of Hohenheim. Aim of the research was to assess the environmental impacts of biogas production at four biogas plants, which were virtually designed with concern to the CHP engine size and the composition of the feedstock. The FU was defined as production of a quantity of biogas with a calorific value of 1 MJ and utilization in a CHP to generate heat and electricity. The whole biogas system was in the focus of the examination to have the basis for a com- parison with an upgrading of biogas to biomethane and other utilization pathways compared to CHP. Therefore, the system boundaries encompass the supply of the energy crops, usage of biogas digestate as an organic fertilizer, and utilization of the biogas. The time frame was 12 months in the year 2009. In Ger- many, agricultural biogas plants use manure as well as energy crops as input substrates. A research at 61 biogas plants showed that maize silage is used by more than 95% and grass silage by more than 50% of the plants under research and liquid cattle manure is the most common manure input with a frequency of utilization of almost 50% [22]. The variety of biogas plants in respect to input substrates ranges from a utilization of liquid manure as a single substrate on one hand to maize silage as single substrate on the other hand. Therefore, the model biogas plants of this study represent typical biogas plants in Germany and are shortly characterized with the technical data of the CHP and mass shares of the feedstock substrates in Table 1 as well as the data for feedstock composition in Table 2.

11: Primary data was used concerning gaseous emissions from mea- surements at a research biogas plant with a 186 kWe (kilo- watt electrical power) biogas engine [25]. Based on these mea- surements, a methane emission of 1.58% of the total methane production was determined. Secondary data was taken from ecoinvent-database (Version 2.0) mainly in regard to the produc- tion of energy crops used as feedstock, the substituted electrical and thermal energy as well as for mineral fertilizer production andtransport processes [26,27].
12: The ISO 14044 standard refers a stepwise procedure to deal with the different possibilities for allocation. An allocation is necessary to assign inputs and outputs of a product system to different products, respectively one main product and several co-products. The stepwise procedure in the ISO standard starts with the suggestion to avoid allocation by dividing the processes or expand the system to include the additional functions of the co-products. Allocation should only be used if this procedure is not possible or meaningful [24]. In this study allocation was avoided by a so-called credit approach. This means that e.g. the fossil based energy generation plants, which are substituted by electricity and heat generation from biogas are taken into con- sideration. Emissions that would be caused by those systems are therefore credited to the biogas system. In Germany, electricity generated from biogas is assumed to substitute the marginal mix of conventional power generation based on the fossil fuels lignite, anthracite, natural gas, and mineral oil with a share of 1%, 66%, 32%, and 1%, respectively (established for 2007). For heat gen- erated from biogas the marginal mix is based on the fossil fuels mineral oil, natural gas, and lignite with a share of 48%, 46%, and 6%, respectively [5]. In this study the marginal energy mix was used and not the average mix because neither nuclear power nor renewable power of the power grid mix is substituted by electric- ity generated from biogas. Therefore, the marginal electricity mix was chosen and not the average electricity mix as in most other studies about LCA of biogas production systems. A credit was also given for emissions of the avoided manure storage. Liquid manure has a potential to produce 0.172 kg CH4/kg organic dry

matter (oDM) during storage in open tanks and 15% out of this is converted to CH4 and emitted to air [28]. Based on these values, the credit for avoided CH4 emissions from manure storage was calculated. Furthermore it was assumed based on Schäfer and IPCC that 15% of NH4-N is converted into NH3-N and 0.1% of total nitrogen in liquid manure is converted into N2O-N during storage [28-30]. To credit the mineral fertilizer that is replaced by the plant available nutrients of the biogas digestate, statistical data of the German Federal Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Consumer Protection was used which names the most important domestic sales regarding mineral fertilizers in Germany in the business year 2007/2008 [31]. When considering straight fertiliz- ers these are: calcium ammonium nitrate for N, super phosphate for P2O5, and potassium chloride for K2O. A share of 80% for N and 100% for P2O5 and K2O is supposed to be plant available [32,33]. The part of digestate, which originates from the share of manure in the feedstock is used as fertilizer in fodder production and hence is excluded from this credit. This is justified by the fact that this organic fertilizer would be applied also without biogas production and consequently, no additional nutrients are pro- vided in the system by this part of the digestate. Field emissions from biogas digestate are calculated as 30% of NH4-N as NH3 [28,34 and 1% of remaining N as N2O [35, 36], while diffuse CH4-emissions from the digester are assumed to be 1% as a con- servative assumption based on measurements from Liebetrau et al. [23]. This assumption is also in line with other LCA studies on biogas production [17,18]. The UBA states that heat produced in biogas fueled CHPs substitutes fossil heat production based on oil, gas, and anthracite coal with a share of 48%, 46%, and 6%, respectively [5]. The electrical power generated is fed into the national electricity grid and grid electricity is used for the electricity requirement of the biogas plant, which was assumed to be 10% of the total electricity produced. The heat requirement was assumed to be 18% of heat produced. Another 10% of the heat produced is supposed to be released to the ambient mainly during summer period while 50% of the remaining heat is used to substitute the marginal heat mix mentioned earlier. The heat as well as electricity demand of biogas plants shows weak cor- relation with the share of manure [22] as well as the volume of the digesters. Nevertheless, a simplification was chosen in this study by using the same values for all model plants in respect of self-energy demand of the model plants. The values chosen are in the range of values commonly measured in practice and are therefore considered sufficiently precise for the purpose of this study. Another simplification was used with giving a credit for fallow land. This was done because at present there is no standardized method available to assess direct and indirect land use change for German agriculture.
13: The ISO 14044 standard refers a stepwise procedure to deal with the different possibilities for allocation. An allocation is necessary to assign inputs and outputs of a product system to different products, respectively one main product and several co-products. The stepwise procedure in the ISO standard starts with the suggestion to avoid allocation by dividing the processes or expand the system to include the additional functions of the co-products. Allocation should only be used if this procedure is not possible or meaningful [24]. In this study allocation was avoided by a so-called credit approach. This means that e.g. the fossil based energy generation plants, which are substituted by electricity and heat generation from biogas are taken into con- sideration. Emissions that would be caused by those systems are therefore credited to the biogas system. In Germany, electricity generated from biogas is assumed to substitute the marginal mix of conventional power generation based on the fossil fuels lignite, anthracite, natural gas, and mineral oil with a share of 1%, 66%, 32%, and 1%, respectively (established for 2007). For heat gen- erated from biogas the marginal mix is based on the fossil fuels mineral oil, natural gas, and lignite with a share of 48%, 46%, and 6%, respectively [5]. In this study the marginal energy mix was used and not the average mix because neither nuclear power nor renewable power of the power grid mix is substituted by electric- ity generated from biogas. Therefore, the marginal electricity mix was chosen and not the average electricity mix as in most other studies about LCA of biogas production systems. A credit was also given for emissions of the avoided manure storage. Liquid manure has a potential to produce 0.172 kg CH4/kg organic dry

matter (oDM) during storage in open tanks and 15% out of this is converted to CH4 and emitted to air [28]. Based on these values, the credit for avoided CH4 emissions from manure storage was calculated. Furthermore it was assumed based on Schäfer and IPCC that 15% of NH4-N is converted into NH3-N and 0.1% of total nitrogen in liquid manure is converted into N2O-N during storage [28-30]. To credit the mineral fertilizer that is replaced by the plant available nutrients of the biogas digestate, statistical data of the German Federal Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Consumer Protection was used which names the most important domestic sales regarding mineral fertilizers in Germany in the business year 2007/2008 [31]. When considering straight fertiliz- ers these are: calcium ammonium nitrate for N, super phosphate for P2O5, and potassium chloride for K2O. A share of 80% for N and 100% for P2O5 and K2O is supposed to be plant available [32,33]. The part of digestate, which originates from the share of manure in the feedstock is used as fertilizer in fodder production and hence is excluded from this credit. This is justified by the fact that this organic fertilizer would be applied also without biogas production and consequently, no additional nutrients are pro- vided in the system by this part of the digestate. Field emissions from biogas digestate are calculated as 30% of NH4-N as NH3 [28,34 and 1% of remaining N as N2O [35, 36], while diffuse CH4-emissions from the digester are assumed to be 1% as a con- servative assumption based on measurements from Liebetrau et al. [23]. This assumption is also in line with other LCA studies on biogas production [17,18]. The UBA states that heat produced in biogas fueled CHPs substitutes fossil heat production based on oil, gas, and anthracite coal with a share of 48%, 46%, and 6%, respectively [5]. The electrical power generated is fed into the national electricity grid and grid electricity is used for the electricity requirement of the biogas plant, which was assumed to be 10% of the total electricity produced. The heat requirement was assumed to be 18% of heat produced. Another 10% of the heat produced is supposed to be released to the ambient mainly during summer period while 50% of the remaining heat is used to substitute the marginal heat mix mentioned earlier. The heat as well as electricity demand of biogas plants shows weak cor- relation with the share of manure [22] as well as the volume of the digesters. Nevertheless, a simplification was chosen in this study by using the same values for all model plants in respect of self-energy demand of the model plants. The values chosen are in the range of values commonly measured in practice and are therefore considered sufficiently precise for the purpose of this study. Another simplification was used with giving a credit for fallow land. This was done because at present there is no standardized method available to assess direct and indirect land use change for German agriculture.
14: Despite the fact that older biogas plants in Germany are typ- ically equipped with open digestate storage tanks, the biogas model plants in this study are covered gas-proof to represent new plants, where mostly gas-tight covers are used. The sensi- tivity of methane emissions from digestate storage on the total GWP of the biogas system was taken into account to assess open as well as gas-tight storage of digestate.

15: Besides GWP, the impact categories eutrophication potential (EP), and acidification potential (AP) have been investigated in this research with a method developed by the Centrum foor Milieukunde in Leiden, Netherlands (CML) [37] using GaBi software (PE International AG).
16: The results showed that GHG emissions can be reduced by using agricultural feedstock for anaerobic digestion and the emerging biogas in a CHP, where the final products are heat and elec- tricity (Fig. 1). The amount of GHG savings depends on feed- stock type. Liquid manure is more advantageous compared to energy crops when digested in small biogas plants as a single input substrate. In this case (plant A) a GHG reduction of 200 g CO2 eq./MJ (carbon dioxide equivalents/MJ) can be realized, while all other plants showed a reduction potential of 78-82 g CO2 eq./MJ. Emission credits as well as burdens contributed to the final result. The highest credit concerning GHG emissions was 107 g CO2 eq./MJ for the avoided manure storage, followed by 91-104 CO2eq./MJ due to the substitution of fossil fuel power plants (based on the marginal electricity mix) by the bio- gas model plants of this study. Furthermore, a significant GHG reduction can be related to the utilization of CHP heat (11-13 CO2 eq./MJ) and the replacement of mineral fertilizers (0-10 g CO2 eq./MJ; Fig. 2). The energy yield from 1t of liquid manure

is about 90% lower than that from the energy crops. This means, that the influence of manure digestion on GWP is very small when liquid manure with shares of 0-35% on a mass basis is used for co-digestion together with energy crops. In biogas plant B liquid manure has a mass share of 35%, while the contribution of liquid manure to biogas energy production is about 5%. As emissions and emission credits of the system under research are expressed in relation to the FU, the credit for avoided manure storage at plant B is only 5% of the credit at plant A where mass share of manure is 100% and thereby equal to share of manure on total biogas energy production of the system. Furthermore, the minor influence of manure used in co-digestion can be ex- plained with the environmental burdens for the production of the energy crops compensating the credit for the avoidance of the manure storage. On the other hand, the overall efficiency of the CHP units' increases with increasing engine size, which resulted in bigger credits for the power generation but to a small extent when compared with the total credit for electricity production. Furthermore, the credit for avoided manure storage is decreasing with the CHP engine size together with the mass of the liquid manure used as a feedstock.

19: Figure 3 indicates that the GWP reduction of the entire process (biogas production including electricity and heat generation) could decrease to 47% of the current amount if the storage tank would not be covered gas-tight at plant A, where only liquid manure is digested. Anyhow savings of GHG emissions in this case are higher as with energy crops in mono-digestion and gas- tight storage of digestate. In plant D the result could change from a reduction of GWP to an additional emission of GHG when assuming the CH4-emissions at digestate storage being 15% of

CH4 produced within the biogas system. Another parameter of considerable influence on the GWP results is the utilization of CHP heat. In Fig. 2 the GWP results are presented for all four model plants with 50% of the surplus heat from the CHP used. If 100% of the CHP surplus heat would be used, additional 12 g CO2 eq./MJ could be saved at all of the four model plants. The results for GWP of the model plants presented here as well as results from an assessment of biogas production at a research plant at practical scale are in the range of other studies performed in Germany, where existing biogas plants were assessed based on on-site measurements [17,21].

These impact categories showed similar results with a reduction of emissions at plant A but increasing emissions at the bigger model biogas plants (Figs. 4 and 5). This fact is mainly caused by the emissions of energy crop production, the CHP and the uti-

Figure 3. Sensitivity of different as- sumptions for CH4-emissions (% of total CH4 production) of digestate storage on global warming potential (GWP) of 50 kWe (kilowatt electrical power) and 2000 kWel model biogas plant.

lization of the digestate as a fertilizer, as well as the comparably low credit for electricity production. Related to EP, the biggest credits were given for the manure storage at plant A and for min- eral fertilizer production at plant B, C, and D. The environmental burdens were caused by the production of energy crops, the di- gestate usage, and the CHP emissions at plants B, C, and D while only the CHP and the power requirement contributed to this impact category at plant A. Energy crops showed a considerably lower contribution to AP at plant D compared to Plants B and C. This can be explained with the emissions that are related with the production of the energy crops. Grass silage shows a four- fold higher AP/kg fresh matter (FM) when compared with maize silage. The results showed that heat and power generation in biogas fed combined heat and power plants under German conditions can reduce potential environmental impacts related to GHG emissions while there was an increase of impacts in the fields of eutrophication and acidification. Only the utilization of liq- uid manure in mono-digestion lead to a reduction of potential impacts in all impact categories under research.

The research demonstrates that anaerobic digestion is a prac- ticable solution for the reduction of GHG emissions related with manure management at small biogas plants with liquid manure in mono- or co-digestion. Furthermore, the generation of electricity from biogas in a combined heat and power plant is advantageous when compared with electricity generation in conventional power plants for both, digestion of liquid manure solely as well as co-digestion with several energy crops. To in- crease reduction of GHG it is helpful to design the digestate storage tanks of biogas plants gas-tight. Nevertheless, further research is needed to determine the site-specific emission rates from uncovered digestate storage tanks because literature values show a wide range. The influence of liquid manure on GWP is very low at bigger biogas plants with shares of 0-35% on a mass basis and does not necessarily lead to an additional reduction of GHG emissions per FU when compared with mono-digestion of energy crops. Nevertheless, the digestion of manures in biogas plants is advantageous in terms of AP and EP and also GHG
Figure 5. Acidification potential (AP) of model biogas plants of different sizes.

emissions from manure storage are avoided. Consequently, an incentive to encourage anaerobic digestion of liquid manure is meaningful. But it should be elaborated in a different form than in the current EEG in Germany, where the incentive is linked with the relative mass share of manure in relation to the total input. A better solution can be for instance to link the financial incentive with the absolute mass of manure, not the relative mass share.

26: Life cycle assessment is a tool for the assessment of prod- ucts or services from a holistic point of view. This publica- tion focuses on the utilization of biogas for power and heat production in combined heat and power plants (CHPs). The Renewable Energies Source Act (EEG) 2009 had the aim to promote renewable energies in Germany. One main purpose of this law is to optimize energy provision for a better protection of climate and environment. A financial

incentive is provided for the utilization of at least 30% of manure as feedstock in biogas plants. The environmen- tal impact of this incentive for manure digestion has not been studied in detail by scientific means. Therefore, the results of this study are of considerable importance for academics as well as the government. Furthermore, this paper will have an impact on biogas practice. For instance, it is shown that liquid manure is more advantageous com- pared to energy crops when digested in biogas plants as a single input substrate; however, the influence of liquid manure on the global warming potential, is small at biogas plants with shares of 0-35% on a mass basis and does not necessarily lead to an additional reduction of green- house gas emissions per functional unit when compared with mono-digestion of energy crops.

The authors have declared no conflict of interest.
